text:
Section 35 BNSS, 2023 - Formal Arrest and Notification
Definition: Procedures of arrest by police officers. The police officer making the arrest must inform the family or nominated person of the arrested person immediately.
Timeline: Within 24 hours of arrest, the details of the arrest must be registered at the District Police Headquarters.
CrPC equivalent: Section 41A/41B CrPC.

Section 36 BNSS, 2023 - Informing Family of Arrested Person
Definition: Obligation of the police officer to inform a relative, friend, or other person nominated by the arrested person about their arrest and the place where they are being held.
Timeline: Immediately upon arrest. Must be recorded in the police station diary.
CrPC equivalent: Section 50A CrPC.

Section 53 BNSS, 2023 - Mandatory Medical Examination of Arrested Person
Definition: Every arrested person must be subjected to a medical examination by a registered medical practitioner (government doctor or private doctor if government doctor is unavailable) immediately after arrest.
Timeline: Within 24 hours of arrest. The medical officer must prepare a detailed injury report and fitness certificate.
CrPC equivalent: Section 54 CrPC.

Section 105 BNSS, 2023 - Mandatory Videography of Search and Seizure
Definition: Search and seizure procedures at the crime scene. The police must record the search and seizure operations (including the preparation of the seizure list/zabti patrak) using electronic/mobile video devices.
Timeline: The recording must be completed during the search operation. The videographed material must be forwarded to the Magistrate without delay to prevent tampering.
CrPC equivalent: Section 100/102 CrPC.

Section 173 BNSS, 2023 - Information in Cognizable Cases and Registration of FIR
Definition: Information relating to the commission of a cognizable offense. Allows filing of FIR via electronic communication (e-FIR), which must be signed by the complainant within three days.
Timeline: Preliminary inquiry for offenses punishable between 3 to 7 years must be completed within 14 days before registering an FIR.
CrPC equivalent: Section 154 CrPC.

Section 180 BNSS, 2023 - Recording of Witness Statements
Definition: Recording of witness statements by investigating officers. Can be recorded in writing or by audio-video electronic means.
Timeline: Statements must be recorded without delay during the course of the investigation.
CrPC equivalent: Section 161 CrPC.

Section 185 BNSS, 2023 - Search by Investigating Officer
Definition: Investigating officer's power to conduct searches of locations within their jurisdiction where they have reasonable grounds to believe evidence is located.
CrPC equivalent: Section 165 CrPC.

Section 187 BNSS, 2023 - Magistrate's Custody Remand
Definition: Remand of accused when investigation cannot be completed within 24 hours of arrest. The Magistrate can authorize detention in police custody or judicial custody.
Timeline: Presentation before Magistrate must be within 24 hours of arrest. Total police custody remand can be requested up to 15 days (extended to 60 or 90 days for chargesheet filing).
CrPC equivalent: Section 167 CrPC.

Section 193 BNSS, 2023 - Filing of Chargesheet
Definition: Form of report to be submitted by the investigating officer to the Magistrate upon completion of the investigation.
Timeline: The chargesheet must be filed within 60 days (for offenses punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years) or 90 days (for offenses punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment for more than 10 years) from the date of arrest/detention.
CrPC equivalent: Section 173 CrPC.

Section 303 BNS, 2023 - Punishment for Theft
Definition: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.
Penalty: Imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both. For second convictions, the punishment is more severe.
IPC equivalent: Section 379 IPC.

Section 305 BNS, 2023 - Theft in Dwelling House, Means of Transportation, or Place of Worship
Definition: Whoever commits theft in any building, tent or vessel, which building, tent or vessel is used as a human dwelling, or for the custody of property, or commits theft in any public transport or place of worship.
Penalty: Imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
IPC equivalent: Section 380 IPC.

Section 331 BNS, 2023 - Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking by Night
Definition: Whoever commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night, in order to the committing of any offense punishable with imprisonment.
Penalty: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fourteen years, and shall also be liable to fine.
IPC equivalent: Section 457 IPC.

Section 101 BNS, 2023 - Definition of Murder
Definition: Except in the cases hereinafter excepted, culpable homicide is murder, if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing death, or causing bodily injury knowing it is likely to cause death, or if it is imminently dangerous that it must in all probability cause death.
IPC equivalent: Section 300 IPC.

Section 103 BNS, 2023 - Punishment for Murder
Definition: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
IPC equivalent: Section 302 IPC.

Section 115 BNS, 2023 - Voluntarily Causing Hurt
Definition: Whoever does any act with the intention of thereby causing hurt to any person, or with the knowledge that he is likely thereby to cause hurt to any person, and does thereby cause hurt to any person, is said voluntarily to cause hurt.
Penalty: Imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees, or with both.
IPC equivalent: Section 323 IPC.

Section 117 BNS, 2023 - Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt
Definition: Voluntarily causing hurt which results in permanent privation of sight or hearing, permanent disfiguration of face/head, fracture or dislocation of bone or tooth, or hurts that endanger life.
Penalty: Imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
IPC equivalent: Section 325 IPC.

Section 318 BNS, 2023 - Cheating and Dishonestly Inducing Delivery of Property
Definition: Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property.
Penalty: Imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
IPC equivalent: Section 420 IPC.

Section 336 BNS, 2023 - Forgery of Documents or Electronic Records
Definition: Whoever makes any false document or false electronic record or part of a document or electronic record, with intent to cause damage or injury to the public or any person, or to support any claim or title, or to cause any person to part with property, or to enter into any express or implied contract, or with intent to commit fraud or that fraud may be committed, commits forgery.
Penalty: Imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
IPC equivalent: Section 468 IPC / Section 465 IPC.

Section 26 BSA, 2023 - Admissibility of Dying Declarations
Definition: Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence. Statements made by a person as to the cause of his death or as to any of the circumstances of the transaction which resulted in his death.
Relevance: Admissible in cases in which the cause of that person's death comes into question.
Evidence Act equivalent: Section 32(1) Indian Evidence Act (IEA).

Section 45 BSA, 2023 - Opinion of Experts
Definition: When the Court has to form an opinion upon a point of foreign law, science, or art, or as to identity of handwriting or finger impressions, the opinions upon that point of persons specially skilled in such foreign law, science, art, or identify of handwriting or finger impressions are relevant facts.
Examples: Medical opinions by doctors on injury status, forensic autopsy reports, ballistics, handwriting comparisons.
Evidence Act equivalent: Section 45 IEA.

Section 63 BSA, 2023 - Admissibility of Electronic Records / Secondary Evidence
Definition: Rules governing secondary evidence and electronic records. Secondary evidence includes printouts, digital scans, photos, videos, and data stored in computer memory, hard drives, or mobile phones.
Relevance: Standardizes the certificate requirements for digital evidence. A digital scan of an FIR, handwritten note, or videographed seizure operation is admissible under this section.
Evidence Act equivalent: Section 63 / 65B IEA.

Section 103 BSA, 2023 - Burden of Proof as to Particular Fact
Definition: The burden of proof as to any particular fact lies on that person who wishes the Court to believe in its existence, unless it is provided by any law that the proof of that fact shall lie on any particular person.
Evidence Act equivalent: Section 103 IEA.

Section 114 BSA, 2023 (Illustration a) - Presumption of Possession of Stolen Goods
Definition: The Court may presume that a man who is in possession of stolen goods soon after the theft is either the thief or has received the goods knowing them to be stolen, unless he can account for his possession.
Evidence Act equivalent: Section 114 Illus (a) IEA.

State of Maharashtra v. Vishwanath (1979) 4 SCC 23
Precedent: The Supreme Court held that temporary removal of property out of the possession of the owner without their consent with a dishonest intention constitutes the offense of theft. The intent to permanently dispossess is not necessary to prove theft; even temporary dispossession is sufficient.
Keywords: theft, steal, gold, ornaments, temporary removal.

K. N. Mehra v. State of Rajasthan, AIR 1957 SC 369
Precedent: The Supreme Court clarified that the 'moving' of a movable property out of the possession of any person with a dishonest intention is the essence of theft. Taking an aircraft for an unauthorized flight was held to constitute theft even if there was no intention to keep the aircraft permanently.
Keywords: theft, dishonest intention, moving property, consent.

K. M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra, AIR 1962 SC 605
Precedent: The Supreme Court laid down the guidelines and tests for determining what constitutes "grave and sudden provocation" and when a murder charge can be reduced to culpable homicide not amounting to murder. It ruled that the provocation must be such that the person loses control of their mind and acts under a sudden impulse, and there must not be sufficient time for cooling down.
Keywords: murder, homicide, grave sudden provocation, cooling down.

State of U.P. v. Deoman Upadhyaya, AIR 1960 SC 1125
Precedent: The Supreme Court upheld the constitutional validity of discovery statements made while in the custody of a police officer. It ruled that facts discovered in consequence of information received from an accused in police custody are admissible.
Keywords: discovery statement, custody, police officer, evidence.

Lalita Kumari v. Govt of U.P. (2014) 2 SCC 1
Precedent: The Supreme Court ruled that registration of an FIR is mandatory under Section 154 of the CrPC (now Section 173 of the BNSS) if the information disclosed to the police officer discloses the commission of a cognizable offense. In such cases, no preliminary inquiry is permissible.
Keywords: FIR registration, mandatory, cognizable offense, preliminary inquiry.

State of Haryana v. Bhajan Lal, 1992 Supp (1) SCC 335
Precedent: The Supreme Court laid down exhaustive guidelines and categories where the High Court can exercise its power under Section 482 of the CrPC (quashing of FIR/complaint) to prevent abuse of the process of any court or otherwise to secure the ends of justice. Relevant for cases of fraud or cheating which are civil in nature.
Keywords: quash FIR, abuse of process, cheating, civil dispute, fraud.

State of Karnataka v. Shivalingaiah (1988) SCC (Cri) 279
Precedent: The Supreme Court clearly differentiated between voluntarily causing hurt/grievous hurt and attempt to commit murder, emphasizing that the nature of the weapon, the force used, and the site of injury on the body must be analyzed to determine the exact intent.
Keywords: assault, bodily hurt, grievous hurt, weapon, murder attempt.
